nodes.name: Composition of Injections Is Injective
nodes.question: State the proposition: Composition of Injections Is Injective.
nodes.statement_display:
$\operatorname{Injective}(f)\wedge\operatorname{Injective}(g)
\Rightarrow\operatorname{Injective}(g\circ f)$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$\operatorname{Injective}(f)\wedge\operatorname{Injective}(g)
\Rightarrow\operatorname{Injective}(g\circ f)$.
nodes.proposition:
\[
  \operatorname{Injective}(g\circ f)
  \;\colonequiv\;
  \forall a_1,a_2\in A:
  (g\circ f)(a_1)=(g\circ f)(a_2)\Rightarrow a_1=a_2.
\]
nodes.source_text:
Proof: $g(f(a_1))=g(f(a_2))\Rightarrow f(a_1)=f(a_2)$ by
$\operatorname{Injective}(g)$, then $a_1=a_2$ by
$\operatorname{Injective}(f)$.
nodes.text_preview: [Composition of Injections Is Injective] $ (f) (g) (g f)$.

nodes.name: Composition of Bijections Is Bijective
nodes.question: State the corollary: Composition of Bijections Is Bijective.
nodes.statement_display:
$\operatorname{Bijective}(f)\wedge\operatorname{Bijective}(g)
\Rightarrow\operatorname{Bijective}(g\circ f)$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$\operatorname{Bijective}(f)\wedge\operatorname{Bijective}(g)
\Rightarrow\operatorname{Bijective}(g\circ f)$.
nodes.text_preview: [Composition of Bijections Is Bijective] $ (f) (g) (g f)$.

nodes.name: Algebra of Limits of Functions
nodes.question: State the theorem: Algebra of Limits of Functions.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f,g:A\to\mathbb{R}$, $c$ a cluster point of $A$, $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f,c,L)$, $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(g,c,M)$. Then:
  1. [label=(\roman*)]
  2. $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f+g,\,c,\,L+M)$.
  3. $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f-g,\,c,\,L-M)$.
  4. $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(\alpha f,\,c,\,\alpha L)$ for all $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$.
  5. $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(fg,\,c,\,LM)$.
  6. If $M\neq 0$: $g(x)\neq 0$ for all $x$ in some punctured neighbourhood of $c$, and $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f/g,\,c,\,L/M)$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $f,g:A\to\mathbb{R}$, $c$ a cluster point of $A$,
$\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f,c,L)$, $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(g,c,M)$.
Then:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
 \item $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f+g,\,c,\,L+M)$.
 \item $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f-g,\,c,\,L-M)$.
 \item $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(\alpha f,\,c,\,\alpha L)$
 for all $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$.
 \item $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(fg,\,c,\,LM)$.
 \item If $M\neq 0$: $g(x)\neq 0$ for all $x$ in some punctured
 neighbourhood of $c$, and
 $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f/g,\,c,\,L/M)$.
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: $\varepsilon$-Closeness of a Function to a Value
nodes.question: State the definition: $\varepsilon$-Closeness of a Function to a Value.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : X \to \mathbb{R}$, let
$L \in \mathbb{R}$, and let $\varepsilon > 0$. The function $f$ is
\textbf{$\varepsilon$-close to $L$} if
$\operatorname{OutputTolerance}(f(x), L, \varepsilon)$ holds for all
$x \in X$, i.e., $|f(x) - L| \leq \varepsilon$ for all $x \in X$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : X \to \mathbb{R}$, let
$L \in \mathbb{R}$, and let $\varepsilon > 0$. The function $f$ is
\textbf{$\varepsilon$-close to $L$} if
$\operatorname{OutputTolerance}(f(x), L, \varepsilon)$ holds for all
$x \in X$, i.e., $|f(x) - L| \leq \varepsilon$ for all $x \in X$.
nodes.source_text:
A global condition: every output lies within $\varepsilon$ of $L$,
regardless of where in $X$ the input lies. This is Tao's scaffolding
toward the limit definition, which localises the same output
condition to a punctured neighbourhood of $c$.
nodes.text_preview: [$ $-Closeness of a Function to a Value] Let $X $, let $f : X $, let $L $, and let $ > 0$. The function $f$ is if $ (f(x), L, )$ holds for all $x X$, i.e., $|f(x) - L| $ for all $x X$.

nodes.name: Local $\delta$-Closeness Near a Point
nodes.question: State the definition: Local $\delta$-Closeness Near a Point.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : X \to \mathbb{R}$, let $L \in \mathbb{R}$, let $x_0$ be
adherent to $X$, and let $\varepsilon, \delta > 0$. The function $f$
is \textbf{$\varepsilon$-close to $L$ near $x_0$ within $\delta$} if
\[
 \forall x \in X :\;
 \operatorname{Within}(x, x_0, \delta)
 \;\Rightarrow\;
 \operatorname{OutputTolerance}(f(x), L, \varepsilon).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $f : X \to \mathbb{R}$, let $L \in \mathbb{R}$, let $x_0$ be
adherent to $X$, and let $\varepsilon, \delta > 0$. The function $f$
is \textbf{$\varepsilon$-close to $L$ near $x_0$ within $\delta$} if
\[
 \forall x \in X :\;
 \operatorname{Within}(x, x_0, \delta)
 \;\Rightarrow\;
 \operatorname{OutputTolerance}(f(x), L, \varepsilon).
\]
nodes.source_text:
A local condition: only outputs from the $\delta$-ball around $x_0$
need lie within $\varepsilon$ of $L$. The input condition here is
$\operatorname{Within}$ — unpunctured. The limit definition replaces
this with $\operatorname{InputTolerance}$ (punctured) and asserts
that for every $\varepsilon$ such a $\delta$ exists.
depends_on_titles: $\varepsilon$-Closeness of a Function to a Value
nodes.text_preview: [Local $ $-Closeness Near a Point] Let $f : X $, let $L $, let $x_0$ be adherent to $X$, and let $ , > 0$. The function $f$ is if \[ x X :\; (x, x_0, ) \; \; (f(x), L, ). \]

nodes.name: Limit of a Function

Three Equivalent Forms of the Function Limit
nodes.text_preview: [Limit of a Function] Let $f : A $ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. A real number $L$ is the , written $ _ x c f(x) = L$, if \[ ,\; ,\; x A :\; (x, c, ) \; \; (f(x), L, ). \] If no such $L$ exists, $f$ at $c$.

nodes.name: Sequential Definition of Limit
nodes.question: State the definition: Sequential Definition of Limit.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
The limit $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$ in the \textbf{sequential
sense} holds if for every sequence $(x_n) \subseteq A \setminus \{c\}$
with $x_n \to c$, the sequence $(f(x_n))$ converges to $L$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
The limit $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$ in the \textbf{sequential
sense} holds if for every sequence $(x_n) \subseteq A \setminus \{c\}$
with $x_n \to c$, the sequence $(f(x_n))$ converges to $L$.
nodes.source_text:
The sequence $(x_n)$ must lie in $A \setminus \{c\}$: the term
$x_n = c$ is excluded, mirroring the $0 <$ in the $\varepsilon$-$\delta$
form. The sequential form is the primary tool for both proving limits
(via the bridge principle) and disproving them (exhibit one bad
sequence). The $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ form is better for constructing
explicit $\delta$ functions.
nodes.text_preview: [Sequential Definition of Limit] Let $f : A $ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. The limit $ _ x c f(x) = L$ in the holds if for every sequence $(x_n) A \ c\ $ with $x_n c$, the sequence $(f(x_n))$ converges to $L$.

nodes.name: Neighbourhood Form of the Limit
nodes.question: State the proposition: Neighbourhood Form of the Limit.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. The following are equivalent:
  1. [label=(\roman*)]
  2. $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$.
  3. For every $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood $V_\varepsilon(L)$ there exists a $\delta$-neighbourhood $V_\delta(c)$ such that $x \in V_\delta^*(c) \cap A \Rightarrow f(x) \in V_\varepsilon(L)$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
The following are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
 \item $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$.
 \item For every $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood $V_\varepsilon(L)$
 there exists a $\delta$-neighbourhood $V_\delta(c)$ such that
 $x \in V_\delta^*(c) \cap A \Rightarrow f(x) \in V_\varepsilon(L)$.
\end{enumerate}
nodes.source_text:
The equivalence is purely notational: $V_\delta^*(c) \cap A$ is
exactly the set of $x \in A$ satisfying
$\operatorname{InputTolerance}(x,c,\delta)$, and $f(x) \in
V_\varepsilon(L)$ is exactly $\operatorname{OutputTolerance}(f(x),L,
\varepsilon)$. The neighbourhood form makes the geometric content
explicit: the image of every punctured $\delta$-slice of $A$ must
fit inside the $\varepsilon$-ball around $L$. This is the form of
continuity used in metric space topology.
depends_on_titles: $\varepsilon$-Closeness of a Function to a Value

nodes.name: Uniqueness of Limits of Functions
nodes.question: State the theorem: Uniqueness of Limits of Functions.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
Then $f$ has at most one limit at $c$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
Then $f$ has at most one limit at $c$.
nodes.source_text:
The limit, when it exists, is uniquely determined by the local
behavior of $f$ near $c$. Uniqueness is what licenses writing
$\lim_{x\to c} f(x) = L$ as an equation rather than an assertion.
nodes.text_preview: [Uniqueness of Limits of Functions] Let $f : A $ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. Then $f$ has at most one limit at $c$.

nodes.name: Sequential Criterion for Function Limits
nodes.question: State the proposition: Sequential Criterion for Function Limits.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. The following are equivalent:
  1. [label=(\alph*)]
  2. $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$.
  3. For every $(x_n) \subseteq A \setminus \{c\}$ with $x_n \to c$, $\; f(x_n) \to L$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
The following are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
 \item $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$.
 \item For every $(x_n) \subseteq A \setminus \{c\}$ with
 $x_n \to c$, $\; f(x_n) \to L$.
\end{enumerate}
depends_on_titles: $\varepsilon$-Closeness of a Function to a Value

nodes.name: Three Equivalent Forms of the Function Limit
nodes.question: State the corollary: Three Equivalent Forms of the Function Limit.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. The following are equivalent:
  1. [label=(\roman*)]
  2. $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f,c,L)$. \hfill ($\varepsilon$-$\delta$)
  3. $\forall V_\varepsilon(L),\;\exists V_\delta(c) :\; x \in V_\delta^*(c) \cap A \Rightarrow f(x) \in V_\varepsilon(L)$. \hfill (neighbourhood)
  4. $\forall (x_n) \subseteq A \setminus \{c\},\; x_n \to c \Rightarrow f(x_n) \to L$. \hfill (sequential)
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
The following are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
 \item $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f,c,L)$.
 \hfill ($\varepsilon$-$\delta$)
 \item $\forall V_\varepsilon(L),\;\exists V_\delta(c) :\;
 x \in V_\delta^*(c) \cap A
 \Rightarrow f(x) \in V_\varepsilon(L)$.
 \hfill (neighbourhood)
 \item $\forall (x_n) \subseteq A \setminus \{c\},\;
 x_n \to c \Rightarrow f(x_n) \to L$.
 \hfill (sequential)
\end{enumerate}
nodes.source_text:
All three encodings capture the same mathematical condition. The
$\varepsilon$-$\delta$ form is best for proving explicit bounds. The
neighbourhood form is best for geometric reasoning and metric space
generalisation. The sequential form is best for importing sequence
results and constructing counterexamples.

nodes.name: Limit Implies Local Boundedness
nodes.question: State the theorem: Limit Implies Local Boundedness.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
If $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f, c, L)$ for some $L \in \mathbb{R}$,
then $\operatorname{BoundedNear}(f, c)$: there exist $\delta, M > 0$
such that $|f(x)| \leq M$ for all $x \in A$ with
$\operatorname{InputTolerance}(x,c,\delta)$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
If $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f, c, L)$ for some $L \in \mathbb{R}$,
then $\operatorname{BoundedNear}(f, c)$: there exist $\delta, M > 0$
such that $|f(x)| \leq M$ for all $x \in A$ with
$\operatorname{InputTolerance}(x,c,\delta)$.
nodes.source_text:
A finite limit does more than control the output near $L$: it
prevents the function from growing without bound near $c$. Local
boundedness is a coarser statement than limit existence, but it is
what is needed for the product and quotient rules.
nodes.text_preview: [Limit Implies Local Boundedness] Let $f : A $ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. If $ (f, c, L)$ for some $L $, then $ (f, c)$: there exist $ , M > 0$ such that $|f(x)| M$ for all $x A$ with $ (x,c, )$.

nodes.name: Bounded Limits
nodes.question: State the theorem: Bounded Limits.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$, and
let $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$. If $a \leq f(x) \leq b$ for all $x \in A$
with $x \neq c$, and $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f,c,L)$, then
$a \leq L \leq b$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$, and
let $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$. If $a \leq f(x) \leq b$ for all $x \in A$
with $x \neq c$, and $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f,c,L)$, then
$a \leq L \leq b$.
nodes.formal:
\[
  \bigl(\forall x \in A \setminus \{c\} :\; a \leq f(x) \leq b\bigr)
  \;\wedge\;
  \operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f,c,L)
  \;\Rightarrow\;
  a \leq L \leq b.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
  \bigl(\forall x \in A \setminus \{c\} :\; a \leq f(x) \leq b\bigr)
  \;\wedge\;
  \operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f,c,L)
  \;\Rightarrow\;
  a \leq L \leq b.
\]
nodes.source_text:
If every nearby value of $f$ is trapped in $[a,b]$, the limit cannot
escape outside $[a,b]$. This is the function-limit analogue of the
sequence result that limits preserve non-strict inequalities. The
proof applies the Sequential Criterion and that sequence result
directly.
nodes.text_preview: [Bounded Limits] Let $f : A $, let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$, and let $a, b $. If $a f(x) b$ for all $x A$ with $x c$, and $ (f,c,L)$, then $a L b$.

nodes.name: Squeeze Theorem for Function Limits
nodes.question: State the theorem: Squeeze Theorem for Function Limits.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f, g, h : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of
$A$. Suppose $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f,c,L) =
\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(g,c,L)$, and suppose there exists
$\delta_0 > 0$ such that
\[
 \forall x \in A,\;
 0 < |x - c| < \delta_0
 \;\Rightarrow\;
 f(x) \leq h(x) \leq g(x).
\]
Then $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(h, c, L)$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $f, g, h : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of
$A$. Suppose $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f,c,L) =
\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(g,c,L)$, and suppose there exists
$\delta_0 > 0$ such that
\[
 \forall x \in A,\;
 0 < |x - c| < \delta_0
 \;\Rightarrow\;
 f(x) \leq h(x) \leq g(x).
\]
Then $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(h, c, L)$.
nodes.source_text:
The proof routes entirely through the Sequential Criterion: convert
to sequences, apply the sequential squeeze theorem, convert back. No
new $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ argument is needed. The condition
$f \leq h \leq g$ is only required near $c$, not globally.
nodes.text_preview: [Squeeze Theorem for Function Limits] Let $f, g, h : A $ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. Suppose $ (f,c,L) = (g,c,L)$, and suppose there exists $ _0 > 0$ such that \[ x A,\; 0 < |x - c| < _0 \; \; f(x) h(x) g(x).

nodes.name: Limits Are Local
nodes.question: State the theorem: Limits Are Local.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $E \subseteq X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $x_0$ be a cluster
point of $E$, let $f : X \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $\delta > 0$.
Then
\[
 \operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f\!\restriction_E,\, x_0,\, L)
 \;\iff\;
 \operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f\!\restriction_{E \cap (x_0-\delta,\,
 x_0+\delta)},\, x_0,\, L).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $E \subseteq X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $x_0$ be a cluster
point of $E$, let $f : X \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $\delta > 0$.
Then
\[
 \operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f\!\restriction_E,\, x_0,\, L)
 \;\iff\;
 \operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f\!\restriction_{E \cap (x_0-\delta,\,
 x_0+\delta)},\, x_0,\, L).
\]
nodes.source_text:
The existence and value of $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f, x_0, L)$
depend only on the behavior of $f$ within any fixed neighbourhood of
$x_0$. Points outside that neighbourhood are irrelevant. This
justifies focusing all limit arguments on small balls around $c$
without any loss of generality.
nodes.text_preview: [Limits Are Local] Let $E X $, let $x_0$ be a cluster point of $E$, let $f : X $, and let $ > 0$. Then \[ (f\! _E,\, x_0,\, L) \; \; (f\! _ E (x_0- ,\, x_0+ ) ,\, x_0,\, L). \]

nodes.name: Limit of Absolute Value
nodes.question: State the proposition: Limit of Absolute Value.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
If $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f,c,L)$, then
$\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(|f|, c, |L|)$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
If $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f,c,L)$, then
$\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(|f|, c, |L|)$.
nodes.negation_display:
The converse fails. $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(|f|,c,|L|)$ does
not imply $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f,c,L)$. Counterexample:
$f(x) = \operatorname{sgn}(x)$ at $c = 0$ has $|f(x)| \equiv 1$ so
$\lim|f| = 1$, but $\lim f$ does not exist.
nodes.negation_formal:
The converse fails. $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(|f|,c,|L|)$ does
not imply $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f,c,L)$. Counterexample:
$f(x) = \operatorname{sgn}(x)$ at $c = 0$ has $|f(x)| \equiv 1$ so
$\lim|f| = 1$, but $\lim f$ does not exist.
nodes.text_preview: [Limit of Absolute Value] Let $f : A $ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. If $ (f,c,L)$, then $ (|f|, c, |L|)$.

nodes.name: Composition of Limits
nodes.question: State the theorem: Composition of Limits.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$, let $g : B \to \mathbb{R}$, let $c_1$ be a
cluster point of $A$, and let $c_2 \in \mathbb{R}$ be a cluster
point of $B$. Suppose $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f, c_1, c_2)$
and $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(g, c_2, L)$. If $c_2 \in B$,
assume also that $g(c_2) = L$. Then
$\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(g \circ f, c_1, L)$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to B$, let $g : B \to \mathbb{R}$, let $c_1$ be a
cluster point of $A$, and let $c_2 \in \mathbb{R}$ be a cluster
point of $B$. Suppose $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f, c_1, c_2)$
and $\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(g, c_2, L)$. If $c_2 \in B$,
assume also that $g(c_2) = L$. Then
$\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(g \circ f, c_1, L)$.
nodes.source_text:
Composition of limits requires care precisely because the input
condition is punctured. The extra hypothesis $g(c_2) = L$ bridges the
gap: when $f(x) = c_2$, the output condition $g(f(x)) = g(c_2) = L$
is satisfied directly. This hypothesis is equivalent to requiring $g$
to be continuous at $c_2$ (within $B$).
nodes.text_preview: [Composition of Limits] Let $f : A B$, let $g : B $, let $c_1$ be a cluster point of $A$, and let $c_2 $ be a cluster point of $B$. Suppose $ (f, c_1, c_2)$ and $ (g, c_2, L)$. If $c_2 B$, assume also that $g(c_2) = L$.

nodes.name: Monotone Limit Theorem
nodes.question: State the theorem: Monotone Limit Theorem.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $a < b$ and let $f : (a,b) \to \mathbb{R}$ be monotone and
bounded. Then both one-sided limits exist at every interior point.
For increasing $f$ and every $x_0 \in (a,b)$:
\[
 f(x_0^-) = \sup\{\,f(x) : a < x < x_0\,\},
 \qquad
 f(x_0^+) = \inf\{\,f(x) : x_0 < x < b\,\}.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $a < b$ and let $f : (a,b) \to \mathbb{R}$ be monotone and
bounded. Then both one-sided limits exist at every interior point.
For increasing $f$ and every $x_0 \in (a,b)$:
\[
 f(x_0^-) = \sup\{\,f(x) : a < x < x_0\,\},
 \qquad
 f(x_0^+) = \inf\{\,f(x) : x_0 < x < b\,\}.
\]
nodes.source_text:
Boundedness guarantees the supremum and infimum used as candidate
limits exist. Monotonicity forces convergence toward them from each
side: for increasing $f$, the values $f(x)$ for $x < x_0$ form a
bounded increasing net approaching their supremum. No oscillation is
possible. The theorem establishes that monotone functions can only
have jump discontinuities — never oscillatory ones.
nodes.text_preview: [Monotone Limit Theorem] Let $a < b$ and let $f : (a,b) $ be monotone and bounded. Then both one-sided limits exist at every interior point. For increasing $f$ and every $x_0 (a,b)$: \[ f(x_0^-) = \ \,f(x) : a < x < x_0\

nodes.name: Cauchy Criterion for Function Limits
nodes.question: State the proposition: Cauchy Criterion for Function Limits.
nodes.source_text:
The Cauchy criterion tests limit existence without knowing $L$. Two
outputs from any sufficiently small punctured ball must lie within
$\varepsilon$ of each other — the outputs cluster even if their
common limit is unknown. This is the function-limit analogue of the
Cauchy completeness criterion for sequences, and relies on the
completeness of $\mathbb{R}$ in exactly the same way.
depends_on_titles: $\varepsilon$-Closeness of a Function to a Value
nodes.text_preview: [Cauchy Criterion for Function Limits] Let $f : A $ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. Then $ (f,c,L)$ exists for some $L $ if and only if \[ ,\; ,\; x, y A :\; (x,c, ) \; \; (y,c, ) \; \; (f(x),f(y), ). \]

nodes.name: Sequential Criterion for Right-Hand Limits
nodes.question: State the theorem: Sequential Criterion for Right-Hand Limits.
nodes.statement_display:
$\operatorname{RightLimit}(f,c,L)\iff$ for every
$(x_n)\subseteq A\cap(c,\infty)$ with $x_n\to c$,
$f(x_n)\to L$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$\operatorname{RightLimit}(f,c,L)\iff$ for every
$(x_n)\subseteq A\cap(c,\infty)$ with $x_n\to c$,
$f(x_n)\to L$.

$\operatorname{LeftLimit}(f,c,L)\iff$ for every
$(x_n)\subseteq A\cap(-\infty,c)$ with $x_n\to c$,
$f(x_n)\to L$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$\operatorname{LeftLimit}(f,c,L)\iff$ for every
$(x_n)\subseteq A\cap(-\infty,c)$ with $x_n\to c$,
$f(x_n)\to L$.

nodes.name: Two-Sided Limit via One-Sided Limits
nodes.question: State the theorem: Two-Sided Limit via One-Sided Limits.
nodes.statement_display:
If $c$ is a cluster point of both $A\cap(c,\infty)$ and
$A\cap(-\infty,c)$, then
\[
 \operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f,c,L)
 \;\iff\;
 \operatorname{RightLimit}(f,c,L) \;\wedge\; \operatorname{LeftLimit}(f,c,L).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
If $c$ is a cluster point of both $A\cap(c,\infty)$ and
$A\cap(-\infty,c)$, then
\[
 \operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f,c,L)
 \;\iff\;
 \operatorname{RightLimit}(f,c,L) \;\wedge\; \operatorname{LeftLimit}(f,c,L).
\]
nodes.source_text:
The two-sided limit exists iff both directions agree. The one-sided
limits are finer instruments detecting directional behavior; the
two-sided limit is a coarser summary requiring both to coincide.
nodes.text_preview: [Two-Sided Limit via One-Sided Limits] If $c$ is a cluster point of both $A (c, )$ and $A (- ,c)$, then \[ (f,c,L) \; \; (f,c,L) \; \; (f,c,L). \]

nodes.name: Pointwise Arithmetic Operations
nodes.question: State the definition: Pointwise Arithmetic Operations.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, $f, g : X \to \mathbb{R}$.
\begin{align*}
 (f+g)(x) &:= f(x)+g(x), & (f-g)(x) &:= f(x)-g(x), \\
 (fg)(x) &:= f(x)g(x), & (cf)(x) &:= cf(x), \\
 |f|(x) &:= |f(x)|, & \max(f,g)(x) &:= \max(f(x),g(x)).
\end{align*}
If $g(x)\neq 0$ for all $x\in X$: $(f/g)(x) := f(x)/g(x)$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, $f, g : X \to \mathbb{R}$.

 (fg)(x) &:= f(x)g(x), & (cf)(x) &:= cf(x), \\
 |f|(x) &:= |f(x)|, & \max(f,g)(x) &:= \max(f(x),g(x)).
\end{align*}
If $g(x)\neq 0$ for all $x\in X$: $(f/g)(x) := f(x)/g(x)$.

nodes.name: Bounded Above, Bounded Below, Bounded
nodes.question: State the definition: Bounded Above, Bounded Below, Bounded.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.
  • $f$ \textbf{bounded above}: $\exists M,\;\forall x\in A: f(x)\leq M$.
  • $f$ \textbf{bounded below}: $\exists m,\;\forall x\in A: f(x)\geq m$.
  • $\operatorname{BoundedOn}(f,A)$: $\exists B>0,\;\forall x\in A: |f(x)|\leq B$.
nodes.text_preview: [Bounded Above, Bounded Below, Bounded] Let $A $, $f:A $. $f$ : $ M,\; x A: f(x) M$. $f$ : $ m,\; x A: f(x) m$. $ (f,A)$: $ B>0,\; x A: |f(x)| B$.

nodes.name: Supremum, Infimum, Maximum, Minimum of a Function
nodes.question: State the definition: Supremum, Infimum, Maximum, Minimum of a Function.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.
  • $\sup_{x\in A}f(x) := \sup f(A)$;\quad $\inf_{x\in A}f(x) := \inf f(A)$.
  • $\operatorname{Maximum}(x^*,f(A))$: $x^*\in A$ and $f(x^*)=\sup f(A)$.
  • $\operatorname{Minimum}(x_*,f(A))$: $x_*\in A$ and $f(x_*)=\inf f(A)$.
nodes.text_preview: [Supremum, Infimum, Maximum, Minimum of a Function] Let $A $, $f:A $. $ _ x A f(x) := f(A)$; $ _ x A f(x) := f(A)$. $ (x^*,f(A))$: $x^* A$ and $f(x^*)= f(A)$. $ (x_*,f(A))$: $x_* A$ and $f(x_*)= f(A)$.

nodes.name: Increasing, Decreasing, Monotone, Constant
nodes.question: State the definition: Increasing, Decreasing, Monotone, Constant.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$, $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ nonempty.
  • \textbf{Increasing}: $x_1
  • \textbf{Strictly increasing}: $x_1
  • \textbf{Decreasing}: $x_1
  • \textbf{Strictly decreasing}: $x_1f(x_2)$.
  • $\operatorname{MonotoneFunction}(f,A)$: increasing or decreasing.
  • \textbf{Constant}: $\forall x_1,x_2\in A: f(x_1)=f(x_2)$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$, $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ nonempty.
\begin{itemize}
 \item \textbf{Increasing}: $x_1<x_2\Rightarrow f(x_1)\leq f(x_2)$.
 \item \textbf{Strictly increasing}: $x_1<x_2\Rightarrow f(x_1)<f(x_2)$.
 \item \textbf{Decreasing}: $x_1<x_2\Rightarrow f(x_1)\geq f(x_2)$.
 \item \textbf{Strictly decreasing}: $x_1<x_2\Rightarrow f(x_1)>f(x_2)$.
 \item $\operatorname{MonotoneFunction}(f,A)$: increasing or decreasing.
 \item \textbf{Constant}: $\forall x_1,x_2\in A: f(x_1)=f(x_2)$.
\end{itemize}
nodes.text_preview: [Increasing, Decreasing, Monotone, Constant] Let $f:A $, $A $ nonempty. : $x_1<x_2 f(x_1) f(x_2)$. : $x_1<x_2 f(x_1)<f(x_2)$. : $x_1<x_2 f(x_1) f(x_2)$. : $x_1<x_2 f(x_1)>f(x_2)$. $ (f,A)$: increasing or decreasing. : $

nodes.name: $\varepsilon$-Neighbourhood
nodes.question: State the definition: $\varepsilon$-Neighbourhood.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $x \in \mathbb{R}$ and $\varepsilon > 0$.
\[
 V_\varepsilon(x) := (x - \varepsilon, x + \varepsilon)
 = \{\, y \in \mathbb{R} : \operatorname{Within}(y, x, \varepsilon) \,\}.
\]
The \textbf{deleted $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood} of $x$ is
\[
 V_\varepsilon^*(x) := V_\varepsilon(x) \setminus \{x\}
 = \{\, y \in \mathbb{R} : \operatorname{InputTolerance}(y, x, \varepsilon) \,\}.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $x \in \mathbb{R}$ and $\varepsilon > 0$.
\[
 V_\varepsilon(x) := (x - \varepsilon, x + \varepsilon)
 = \{\, y \in \mathbb{R} : \operatorname{Within}(y, x, \varepsilon) \,\}.
\]
The \textbf{deleted $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood} of $x$ is
\[
 V_\varepsilon^*(x) := V_\varepsilon(x) \setminus \{x\}
 = \{\, y \in \mathbb{R} : \operatorname{InputTolerance}(y, x, \varepsilon) \,\}.
\]

nodes.name: Cluster Point
nodes.question: State the definition: Cluster Point.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and $x \in \mathbb{R}$. The point $x$
is a \textbf{cluster point} of $X$ if
\[
 \forall\varepsilon > 0,\;
 \exists\, y \in X \setminus \{x\} :\;
 \operatorname{Within}(y, x, \varepsilon).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and $x \in \mathbb{R}$. The point $x$
is a \textbf{cluster point} of $X$ if
\[
 \forall\varepsilon > 0,\;
 \exists\, y \in X \setminus \{x\} :\;
 \operatorname{Within}(y, x, \varepsilon).
\]

nodes.name: Sequential Characterization of Cluster Points
nodes.question: State the theorem: Sequential Characterization of Cluster Points.
nodes.statement_display:
$\operatorname{ClusterPoint}(c, A)$ if and only if there exists
$(a_n) \subseteq A \setminus \{c\}$ with $a_n \to c$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$\operatorname{ClusterPoint}(c, A)$ if and only if there exists
$(a_n) \subseteq A \setminus \{c\}$ with $a_n \to c$.
nodes.source_text:
The forward direction: use $\varepsilon = 1/n$ to build the witness
sequence. The reverse direction is immediate since the sequence
witnesses the ball condition for every $\varepsilon$. This bridges
the $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ definition with the sequential language.
nodes.text_preview: [Sequential Characterization of Cluster Points] $ (c, A)$ if and only if there exists $(a_n) A \ c\ $ with $a_n c$.

nodes.name: Adherent Point; Isolated Point
nodes.question: State the definition: Adherent Point; Isolated Point.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and $x \in \mathbb{R}$.

$x$ is an \textbf{adherent point} of $X$ if
\[
 \forall\varepsilon > 0,\;
 \exists\, y \in X :\;
 \operatorname{Within}(y, x, \varepsilon).
\]

A point $x \in X$ is an \textbf{isolated point} of $X$ if
$\neg\,\operatorname{ClusterPoint}(x, X)$; equivalently,
$\exists\varepsilon > 0 : V_\varepsilon(x) \cap X = \{x\}$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and $x \in \mathbb{R}$.

$x$ is an \textbf{adherent point} of $X$ if
\[
 \forall\varepsilon > 0,\;
 \exists\, y \in X :\;
 \operatorname{Within}(y, x, \varepsilon).
\]

A point $x \in X$ is an \textbf{isolated point} of $X$ if
$\neg\,\operatorname{ClusterPoint}(x, X)$; equivalently,
$\exists\varepsilon > 0 : V_\varepsilon(x) \cap X = \{x\}$.

nodes.name: Interior, Exterior, Boundary
nodes.question: State the definition: Interior, Exterior, Boundary.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, $x \in \mathbb{R}$.

$\operatorname{InteriorPoint}(x, X)$: $\exists\varepsilon > 0 :
V_\varepsilon(x) \subseteq X$.

$\operatorname{BoundaryPoint}(x, X)$: $\forall\varepsilon > 0 :
V_\varepsilon(x) \cap X \neq \emptyset \;\wedge\;
V_\varepsilon(x) \cap X^c \neq \emptyset$.

Interior $X^\circ := \{x : \operatorname{InteriorPoint}(x,X)\}$;\quad
boundary $\partial X := \{x : \operatorname{BoundaryPoint}(x,X)\}$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, $x \in \mathbb{R}$.

$\operatorname{InteriorPoint}(x, X)$: $\exists\varepsilon > 0 :
V_\varepsilon(x) \subseteq X$.

$\operatorname{BoundaryPoint}(x, X)$: $\forall\varepsilon > 0 :
V_\varepsilon(x) \cap X \neq \emptyset \;\wedge\;
V_\varepsilon(x) \cap X^c \neq \emptyset$.

Interior $X^\circ := \{x : \operatorname{InteriorPoint}(x,X)\}$;\quad
boundary $\partial X := \{x : \operatorname{BoundaryPoint}(x,X)\}$.
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
  \operatorname{InteriorPoint}(x,X)
  &\;\iff\; \exists\varepsilon>0,\;
    \forall y : \operatorname{Within}(y,x,\varepsilon) \Rightarrow y \in X. \\
  \operatorname{BoundaryPoint}(x,X)
  &\;\iff\; \forall\varepsilon>0 :
    V_\varepsilon(x)\cap X\neq\emptyset
    \wedge V_\varepsilon(x)\cap X^c\neq\emptyset.
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
  \operatorname{InteriorPoint}(x,X)
  &\;\iff\; \exists\varepsilon>0,\;
    \forall y : \operatorname{Within}(y,x,\varepsilon) \Rightarrow y \in X. \\
  \operatorname{BoundaryPoint}(x,X)
  &\;\iff\; \forall\varepsilon>0 :
    V_\varepsilon(x)\cap X\neq\emptyset
    \wedge V_\varepsilon(x)\cap X^c\neq\emptyset.
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.source_text:
$X^\circ$, $\partial X$, $\mathrm{Ext}(X)$ partition $\mathbb{R}$.
$\operatorname{OpenSet}(X) \iff X = X^\circ$.
$\operatorname{ClosedSet}(X) \iff X \supseteq \partial X$.
$\overline{X} = X^\circ \cup \partial X$.
nodes.text_preview: [Interior, Exterior, Boundary] Let $X $, $x $. $ (x, X)$: $ > 0 : V_ (x) X$. $ (x, X)$: $ > 0 : V_ (x) X \; \; V_ (x) X^c $. Interior $X^ := \ x : (x,X)\ $; boundary $ X := \ x : (x,X)\ $.

nodes.statement_display: $\overline{X} := \{x \in \mathbb{R} : \operatorname{AdherentPoint}(x, X)\}$.
nodes.statement_tex: $\overline{X} := \{x \in \mathbb{R} : \operatorname{AdherentPoint}(x, X)\}$.

nodes.name: Closed Subset of $\mathbb{R}$
nodes.question: State the definition: Closed Subset of $\mathbb{R}$.
nodes.statement_display: $\operatorname{ClosedSet}(E) \iff \overline{E} = E$.
nodes.statement_tex: $\operatorname{ClosedSet}(E) \iff \overline{E} = E$.
nodes.text_preview: [Closed Subset of $ $] $ (E) = E$.

nodes.name: Closed iff Sequentially Closed
nodes.question: State the corollary: Closed iff Sequentially Closed.
nodes.statement_display:
$\operatorname{ClosedSet}(X) \iff \forall (a_n)\subseteq X,\;
a_n \to x \Rightarrow x \in X$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$\operatorname{ClosedSet}(X) \iff \forall (a_n)\subseteq X,\;
a_n \to x \Rightarrow x \in X$.
nodes.source_text:
A closed set cannot be escaped by convergent sequences. Every adherent
point has a witness sequence; closedness forces the limit into the set.
nodes.text_preview: [Closed iff Sequentially Closed] $ (X) (a_n) X,\; a_n x x X$.

nodes.name: Every Point of an Interval Is a Cluster Point
nodes.question: State the corollary: Every Point of an Interval Is a Cluster Point.
nodes.statement_display:
If $I$ is an interval, then $\operatorname{ClusterPoint}(x, I)$ for
every $x \in I$.
nodes.statement_tex:
If $I$ is an interval, then $\operatorname{ClusterPoint}(x, I)$ for
every $x \in I$.
nodes.source_text:
This justifies intervals as natural domains for function limits.
$\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f,c,L)$ requires
$\operatorname{ClusterPoint}(c, \operatorname{dom}(f))$; on any
interval this is free.
nodes.text_preview: [Every Point of an Interval Is a Cluster Point] If $I$ is an interval, then $ (x, I)$ for every $x I$.

nodes.name: Bounded Subset of $\mathbb{R}$
nodes.question: State the definition: Bounded Subset of $\mathbb{R}$.
nodes.statement_display:
$\operatorname{BoundedSet}(X) \iff \exists M>0,\;\forall x\in X:
|x|\leq M$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$\operatorname{BoundedSet}(X) \iff \exists M>0,\;\forall x\in X:
|x|\leq M$.
nodes.formal:
\[
  \operatorname{BoundedSet}(X)
  \;\iff\;
  \exists M>0,\;\forall x\in X : |x|\leq M.
  \qquad
  \neg\,\operatorname{BoundedSet}(X)
  \;\iff\;
  \forall M>0,\;\exists x\in X : |x|>M.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
  \operatorname{BoundedSet}(X)
  \;\iff\;
  \exists M>0,\;\forall x\in X : |x|\leq M.
  \qquad
  \neg\,\operatorname{BoundedSet}(X)
  \;\iff\;
  \forall M>0,\;\exists x\in X : |x|>M.
\]
nodes.text_preview: [Bounded Subset of $ $] $ (X) M>0,\; x X: |x| M$.

nodes.name: Heine--Borel Theorem for $\mathbb{R}$
nodes.question: State the theorem: Heine--Borel Theorem for $\mathbb{R}$.
nodes.statement_display:
$\operatorname{ClosedSet}(X) \wedge \operatorname{BoundedSet}(X)$
$\iff$ every $(a_n)\subseteq X$ has a subsequence converging to
a limit in $X$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$\operatorname{ClosedSet}(X) \wedge \operatorname{BoundedSet}(X)$
$\iff$ every $(a_n)\subseteq X$ has a subsequence converging to
a limit in $X$.
nodes.source_text:
Closed prevents limits from escaping through boundary gaps; bounded
prevents escape to infinity. Both are required. Forward: bounded
gives Bolzano--Weierstrass; closed keeps the limit in $X$. Reverse:
negate either hypothesis to construct a sequence with no convergent
subsequence in $X$.
nodes.text_preview: [Heine--Borel Theorem for $ $] $ (X) (X)$ $ $ every $(a_n) X$ has a subsequence converging to a limit in $X$.

nodes.name: True Near a Point
nodes.question: State the definition: True Near a Point.
nodes.statement_display:
Property $P(f(x))$ is \textbf{true near $x_0$} if
$\exists\delta>0,\;\forall x : \operatorname{InputTolerance}(x,x_0,
\delta) \Rightarrow P(f(x))$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Property $P(f(x))$ is \textbf{true near $x_0$} if
$\exists\delta>0,\;\forall x : \operatorname{InputTolerance}(x,x_0,
\delta) \Rightarrow P(f(x))$.
nodes.source_text:
$\operatorname{FunctionLimit}(f,x_0,L)$ states: for every
$\varepsilon>0$, the property $\operatorname{OutputTolerance}(f(x),
L,\varepsilon)$ is true near $x_0$.
